findings.reasoning: Milarna (data-requirements analyst) flagged that Phase 7 ('automatic update of evidence store on model change') does not address data lifecycle governance. Legal reviewers and Safety Engineers have not collaborated on retention, reuse, and destruction policies.
findings.recommendation: Host all-team workshop with T&E Engineer, Safety Engineer, Data Engineer, and Legal Reviewer. Define data-lifecycle policy: retention period, archival rules, DPIA templates, backup SLOs, cross-programme reuse policy. Deliver data-governance document before Phase 2 data curation.

findings.title: Stakeholder governance structure and sign-off authorities undefined — Milarna systemic gap
findings.feature: AMLAS-structured evidence bundle and GSN safety case auto-generation
findings.reasoning: Section 10 names stakeholders (RAISO, DAIC, DAU) and references policies (JSP 520, DEFSTAN 00-056) but does not specify decision rights or escalation paths. Business, Legal, and Safety perspectives have not collaborated to define release-gate logic.
findings.recommendation: Convene governance workshop with T&E Engineer (business), Safety Engineer, Legal Reviewer, and representatives from RAISO/DAU. Define decision authorities, approval levels, escalation paths, and timeline. Produce RACI matrix and release-gate decision tree before Phase 6 begins.

findings.title: Drift-detection SLO escalation workflow not automatable; manual operator judgment required
findings.reasoning: Scenario SCEN-015 requires simulating human operator override decision: 'When the on-watch engineer acknowledges alert but continues operation.' In production, this decision involves human judgment (e.g., 'Is mission-critical? Can we reach safe harbor? Is weather improving?'). Test automation cannot replicate this judgment or predict operator behaviour. Scenario as written assumes operator will always override; in reality, operator might accept re-qualification delay or request controlled shutdown. Automating this scenario as written risks creating unrealistic test that passes in lab but fails in field (operator behaviour differs from test assumption).
findings.recommendation: Reframe SCEN-015 as a specification of system behaviour rather than scenario test: 'When a human operator overrides a SLO-red alert (operator choice outside system scope), the system shall log the override and raise Article 36 exception.' Change test to focus on system's logging and escalation, not on predicting operator override. Test harness injects a simulated 'operator override' signal (flag or command) and verifies system response: (1) override event logged with timestamp and justification, (2) Article 36 exception raised in audit trail, (3) event flagged for post-mission safety review. Move operator-behaviour testing to Phase 5 real-world trials where human factors are observed naturally. For Phase 7 in-service monitoring, create integration test that validates drift-alert → audit-log pipeline, independent of operator override likelihood. Document that Phase 7 automation provides 'operator-in-the-loop' monitoring, not full autonomous re-qualification; human operator retains decision authority.

findings.title: Missing ODD Coverage Heatmap for 'Does System Handle Sea State 5 Fog'
findings.reasoning: A new T&E engineer asking 'Does the system handle Sea State 5 fog adversarial?' has no quick way to find relevant scenarios. Current feature files scattered across 7 phases. No single artifact showing ODD partition coverage (visibility x sea-state x illumination x sensor-mode). Isabel's examples and Rex's refined specs reference specific conditions but no summary view.
findings.recommendation: Create interactive ODD coverage heatmap (HTML/CSV table) showing: (1) visibility ranges (50–500 m, etc.); (2) sea state (Beaufort 2–5); (3) illumination (daylight/dusk/night/fog); (4) sensor modalities (radar/visual/IR/fused); (5) for each cell, list features/scenarios covering that condition. Link cells to feature files and scenario IDs. Update navigation aids to highlight this tool. Populate from grace-goals.json feature definitions and rex-refined.json example data.

findings.title: REQ-009: Scenario volume target vague—'10× more' lacks measurable baseline
findings.feature: OpenSCENARIO 2.0 and Scenic scenario definition and automation
findings.reasoning: User story states '10× more scenarios per engineer-day' but provides no baseline (manual: 5? 10? 50/day?). Success metric must anchor to observed historical velocity (e.g. 'baseline 5 scenarios/day manual → 50 scenarios/day parametric')
findings.recommendation: Establish baseline: query T&E team on current scenario-authoring velocity; restate success metric as 'from X manual scenarios/engineer-day to ≥5X parametric scenarios/engineer-day'

findings.title: REQ-011: GSN auto-generation success metric '95% goal coverage' vague on coverage definition and human refinement effort
findings.feature: AMLAS-structured evidence bundle and GSN safety case auto-generation
findings.reasoning: Success metric: '95% of sub-goals supported by linked evidence' conflates auto-generation quality (did tool generate claims?) with safety-case quality (are claims correct?). User story promises 'weeks to days' but success metric does not track human review+refinement time. Risk: tool generates 95% of claims in 1 hour, but human refinement takes 5 days, yielding no net speedup.
findings.recommendation: Refine success metric: 'Draft GSN generated in <2 hours (tool time). Human review + refinement + approval completes in <2 engineer-days (baseline: 3-7 days). Measure end-to-end time savings, not tool output completeness. Track ratio: (auto-generated + minimally refined claims) / (total claims) ≥90% to validate human refinement is indeed minimal.'

findings.title: Acceptance baseline for speedup claims (3-5×, 10×) undefined — Alexa/Milarna gap
findings.feature: Automated requirements decomposition to test conditions
findings.reasoning: Alexa flagged that '3-5× speedup' and '10× more scenarios per engineer-day' lack baselines and measurement methods. Business (T&E Engineer) and Developer have not agreed on how to measure benefits or what constitutes success.
findings.recommendation: Three-amigos workshop (T&E Engineer, Developer, Data Engineer) to baseline current manual effort (requirements decomposition time, scenario design time, test-case generation time) and define measurement protocol. Establish baseline metrics before pilot run, measure improvements post-deployment.

findings.title: StyleGAN invocation leaks implementation detail into spec
findings.reasoning: Specification should declare business requirement (generate 10k synthetic frames with provenance) not tool selection. StyleGAN is implementation detail; future replacements (DiffusionNet, DiT) must not invalidate the spec.
findings.recommendation: Rephrase as: "Given curated real logs, When 10x augmentation is requested for rare conditions, Then synthetic frames are produced with full provenance tracking"

findings.title: SCEN-001/002 describe decomposition workflow, not specification outcome
findings.reasoning: Original: "T&E Engineer requests automated decomposition to testable conditions" + "pipeline extracts environment variable vectors" = tool workflow. Spec should state: "Given ODD requirement with explicit state boundaries, When decomposition is triggered, Then all declared states are partitioned and linked to test atoms".
findings.recommendation: Strip actor/action sequence; focus on input → output contract

findings.title: Robustness bounds lack stratification by object class and modality
findings.reasoning: SCEN-007 says "robustness certificate for 8/255 perturbations" but chris-language.json mandates "For each object class (vehicle, buoy) and sensor modality, specify certified ε and confidence level". Current spec conflates across classes.
findings.recommendation: Refine: "robustness bounds are filed per object class (merchant vessel, fishing vessel, buoy) and sensor modality (radar, visual, fused)"

findings.title: SCEN-011 handover threshold (70%) assumed but not derived
findings.reasoning: Spec says "confidence falling below handover threshold (70%)" but does not state where 70% comes from. Is it SIL requirement? ODD boundary? Confidence score must be linked to safety argument in traceability matrix.
findings.recommendation: Declare: "confidence threshold determined from [SIL target] and [ODD coverage]. Handover triggered when model confidence or environmental fit score < threshold"

findings.title: SCEN-016-017 describe GSN tooling, not specification outcome
findings.reasoning: Spec says "draft GSN tree is generated" but spec is not about GSN generation tool; it should declare: "Evidence bundle is structured to satisfy AMLAS requirements with bidirectional traceability between safety claims and test evidence".
findings.recommendation: Reframe as: "Given AMLAS evidence (activities 1-6), When safety case assembly is triggered, Then GSN structure links each claim to supporting evidence artifact in traceability matrix"

findings.title: Scenario Outline Examples tables require real maritime data values for validation
findings.reasoning: Scenario Outline Examples tables use placeholder values (visibility_range, sea_state_range) instead of concrete maritime ranges. For Gherkin automation, Examples must contain actual test data: e.g., visibility_range='50–500 m', sea_state_range='Beaufort 2–4'. Without concrete data, test harness cannot bind parameters to assertion thresholds or traceability IDs. Isabel's example data provides concrete values (visibility 50/200/500 m, Beaufort 2–4); these must be embedded into Examples tables.
findings.recommendation: Replace all placeholder values in Scenario Outline Examples with concrete maritime data from Isabel's examples.json. For each phase, extract 2–3 representative ODD scenarios covering: (1) nominal conditions (sea state Beaufort 2–3, visibility 200–500 m, daylight), (2) boundary conditions (sea state Beaufort 4–5, visibility 50–100 m, twilight), (3) rare/adversarial (sea state Beaufort 5–6, visibility <50 m, night). Update Examples tables with data columns: num_environment_vars, visibility_range_min, visibility_range_max, sea_state_min, sea_state_max, illumination_values. Validate that each Examples row maps to a distinct ODD partition and can be traced to a requirement atom.

findings.title: Model metric thresholds (mAP, ECE, robust accuracy) hardcoded in scenarios; SLO not parameterized
findings.reasoning: Scenario Outline defines hardcoded Examples with fixed metric values (92% mAP, 94% precision, 87% recall, ECE=0.038). In practice, acceptable metric thresholds vary by model version, ODD region, sensor modality, and target object class. For example, small-boat detection may require ≥85% mAP on <5 m vessels but ≥92% on merchant vessels. Currently, scenarios cannot adapt to phase-specific SLO updates or per-class metric requirements. Automation harness will fail scenarios if new model achieves 91% mAP on small boats (reasonable within SLO) but Examples expect 92% globally.
findings.recommendation: Parameterize metric thresholds via external SLO configuration file or environment variables. Create SLO profile per object class and sensor modality: {object_class: 'small_boat', sensor: 'radar', min_mAP: 85, min_recall: 80, max_ECE: 0.12}. Update step definitions to load SLO from config; compare model metrics against class-specific thresholds instead of hardcoded Examples values. Scenario Outline Examples should reference SLO profile ID rather than numeric thresholds: 'When the model is evaluated against SLO profile SLO-RADAR-SMALLBOAT'. Link SLO profiles to AMLAS activity 2.3 (verification report) and archive in evidence store. Enable automated SLO updates on model release cycle without scenario rework.

findings.title: Framework recommendation: Multi-language test automation harness for domain-specific layer integration
findings.reasoning: Gherkin feature files are technology-agnostic, but underlying test harness must bridge: (1) Requirements-decomposition engine (Python/Java business logic), (2) ML model evaluation (Python scikit-learn, PyTorch), (3) HIL simulator integration (Robot Framework, ROS 2), (4) Evidence-store queries (REST API or SQL), (5) GSN claim generation (custom ontology reasoning). Recommending single framework (e.g., Cucumber-JVM only) creates bottlenecks: JVM does not natively support PyTorch inference; Robot Framework is weak at statistical testing. Recommending multiple frameworks (Cucumber + pytest + Robot) risks fragmented test execution and loose traceability between phases.
findings.recommendation: Implement composite test harness: (1) Cucumber-JVM as orchestration layer (parses Gherkin, drives phase-gated execution, manages CI/CD gates), (2) pytest-bdd with Python plugins for Phases 1–3 (requirements decomposition, data validation, ML metrics), (3) Robot Framework with ROS 2 integration for Phase 4 (HIL scenarios), (4) Custom AMLAS evidence validator (Python/Java) for Phases 5–6 (gap detection, GSN claim assembly), (5) Drift-detection microservice (Python Evidently AI, Alibi Detect) for Phase 7. Use test hooks and listener APIs to bridge frameworks: Cucumber listener captures phase results and pushes to evidence store; pytest-bdd tests export metrics in OpenMetrics format for Cucumber to aggregate; Robot Framework exports telemetry via JSON. Implement language-agnostic evidence-store API (JSON-RPC or REST) so all frameworks query traceability matrix and log results. This architecture avoids single-framework bottleneck while maintaining unified test orchestration and evidence lineage. Document in angie-automation.json (completed).

findings.title: Terminology Precision Gap: ODD States vs. Environment Variables
findings.reasoning: chris-language.json defines 'declared ODD states' as discrete enumerations/partitions and 'environment variables' as measurable parameters. Examples conflate these concepts, risking test engineer confusion about whether ODD is fixed or continuous. AMLAS activity 1 requires clarity here.
findings.recommendation: Add clarifying section to glossary: 'declared ODD states' = discrete or continuous parameterization defining testable bounds; 'environment variables' = specific measurable attributes monitored during test and deployment. Update EX-001, EX-019 to use correct term. Add cross-reference in feature files 01-requirements-decomposition.

findings.title: Missing AMLAS Activity Cross-Reference Index for Quick Navigation
findings.reasoning: DAIC and York CfAA assurance teams work within AMLAS activity framework. Current structure is PSRQ-phase-first (Phase 1–7). A safety engineer needing 'all evidence for AMLAS activity 3.2 (robustness testing)' must manually map across multiple features (REQ-005, REQ-006, parts of REQ-004). No index. Impacts regulatory sign-off speed.
findings.recommendation: Create AMLAS activity index page (docs/amlas-activity-index/index.md) with table: Activity # | Requirements | Features | Scenarios | Refined Specs | Examples | Evidence Artifacts. Link each cell to corresponding sections. Example row: 'AMLAS 3.2 (Robustness) | REQ-005 | 03-model-evaluation/REQ-005-adversarial-robustness.feature | REF-007–009 | EX-015–018'. Hyperlink all references. Maintain index as living document synced by CI/CD pipeline.

findings.title: No Glossary Backlinks: Hard to Discover Features Using a Specific Term
findings.reasoning: chris-language.json defines 55 terms. Each term lists related_terms but not which features/examples use it. Example: a new engineer wants to find all scenarios involving 'degraded-sensor modes'. Glossary entry exists, but they must manually search all 28 feature files and 40 examples. Discovery is linear, not indexed.
findings.recommendation: Generate glossary page with backlinks: for each term, list features/scenarios/examples that reference it. Example: glossary entry 'degraded-sensor modes' includes: 'Used in: REQ-008 (HIL scenarios, REF-011), REQ-006 (human handover), EX-012, EX-018'. Automate via CI/CD: pipeline scans feature files and examples for glossary terms, builds backlink map, publishes to glossary.md. Enables fast term-to-scenario discovery.

findings.title: CI/CD Evolution Process Must Include Terminology Validation Gate
findings.reasoning: laveena-structure.json recommends CI/CD-driven docs evolution. Without terminology validation gate, new features will introduce ad-hoc terminology. Current codebase has 9 inconsistencies; if feature merges ignore glossary, inconsistencies will accumulate. Safety case will lack precision; AMLAS activity evidence will be ambiguous.
findings.recommendation: Add CI/CD validation step before feature merge: (1) regex-scan new *.feature files against chris-language.json defined terms; (2) flag non-standard usage (e.g., 'evidence' without 'bundle' context, 'sensor mode' without 'modality'); (3) permit non-standard only if explicitly justified in commit message (e.g., 'GLOSSARY-EXCEPTION: new term X approved by Safety Lead'); (4) auto-update glossary if new term is valid; (5) block merge if flag count > 2. Script: .ci/check-terminology.sh (bash regex validator). Integrate into GitHub Actions or GitLab CI pipeline. Report: docs/consistency/consistency-report.md updated on each merge.

findings.title: REQ-003: Synthetic data generation scope creeps into generative model development
findings.feature: Synthetic sensor data generation for rare and adversarial conditions
findings.reasoning: Feature claims 'NVIDIA TAO, StyleGAN, diffusion' for 'rare events and adversarial conditions'. This conflates data augmentation (in-scope: resynthesizing rare real conditions from seeds) with generative modeling (out-of-scope: learning new object classes, novel sensor modalities). Risk: team attempts to build bespoke maritime diffusion models instead of adapting COTS tools to existing data.
findings.recommendation: Clarify scope: 'Synthetic data generation applies COTS StyleGAN/diffusion to augment rare real-world conditions; generation of wholly novel scenarios or sensor modalities out of scope for PSRQ Phase 2. Novel generative models deferred to Phase 2.2 (future investment) if ODD coverage gap detected.'

findings.title: REQ-014: MLOps lineage scope vague on model-equivalence and rollback automation
findings.feature: MLOps lineage and automated evidence store update on model change
findings.reasoning: Feature claims 'auto-update evidence store when model changes' but does not specify: (a) Does evidence-store rollback to prior model's evidence if new model fails re-qualification? (b) Does system auto-rollback deployed model on SLO breach or require human approval? (c) Are model-equivalence checks automated (e.g. via formal methods) or manual human decision? These are critical safety-case assumptions currently implicit.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit scope boundary: 'Evidence-store automatic updates limited to re-running Phase 3–4 tests; model rollback and re-qualification sign-off remain human-driven via safety case author approval. Model-equivalence formal verification out of scope for Phase 7 MVP.'

findings.title: REQ-013: Drift detection '≥3 environment variables' arbitrary—no principled ODD variable selection
findings.feature: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
findings.reasoning: Acceptance criteria state 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables' but does not justify why 3 (vs 2, vs 5). No clear methodology for selecting which ODD variables to monitor (e.g. correlation to model performance? Observability from deployed sensor suite?). Current spec: sea-state, visibility, illumination assumed without validation.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Drift monitor variable selection driven by Phase 1 sensitivity analysis: variables that correlate >0.7 with model accuracy regression are mandatory; optional variables (e.g., illumination) chosen by Sea-Trial Manager based on deployment geography. Phase 7 MVP monitors ≥3 variables; Phase 7.1 (future) scales to ≥5 based on post-deployment data.'

findings.title: REQ-001: Traceability matrix 'zero gaps' unrealistic—no definition of acceptable gap threshold
findings.feature: Automated requirements decomposition to test conditions
findings.reasoning: Success metric: 'traceability matrix achieves 100% ODD-to-atom mapping with zero gaps' is aspirational but risky. Phase 1 output: 'gap detection in underspecified ODDs' explicitly acknowledges underspecification will occur. Claiming 'zero gaps' ignores this reality; instead, should set realistic coverage threshold (e.g. 95%) and escalation procedure for remaining gaps.
findings.recommendation: Revise success metric: 'Traceability matrix achieves ≥95% ODD-to-atom mapping. Remaining 5% gaps flagged in 'issues' register for Phase 1 close-out; gaps either resolved via requirement refinement or accepted as deferred scope with documented risk sign-off.'

findings.title: Rare-event frequency threshold and augmentation ratio undefined — Alexa/Milarna collaboration gap
findings.feature: Synthetic sensor data generation for rare and adversarial conditions
findings.reasoning: Alexa flagged ambiguity on 'rare events' definition; Milarna noted missing data on augmentation targets. T&E Engineer, Tester, and Safety Engineer have not agreed on frequency threshold, augmentation rule, or validation method.
findings.recommendation: Three-amigos workshop (T&E Engineer, Tester, Data Engineer) to define rare-event frequency threshold (e.g., <1% is rare), augmentation ratio policy, and statistical validation method. Deliver rare-event catalogue with frequencies and augmentation ratios before Phase 2 data curation.

findings.title: Drift-detection environment variables and threshold undefined — Alexa/Milarna process gap
findings.feature: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
findings.reasoning: Alexa noted 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables' lacks specification of which variables and selection method. Milarna identified that drift detection triggers re-qualification but the trigger rules and escalation paths are missing. Developer and T&E Engineer have not collaborated on variable selection, threshold tuning, or failure handling.
findings.recommendation: Three-amigos workshop (Developer, T&E Engineer, Data Engineer) to select drift-monitoring variables (baseline: 3–5 ODD parameters), define per-variable drift threshold (statistical test, p-value), aggregate decision logic, and canary-rollback procedures. Deliver drift-monitoring configuration before Phase 7 deployment.

findings.title: Missing availability non-functional requirement for in-service monitoring
findings.feature: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
findings.reasoning: Milarna flagged missing availability SLA for drift detector in Phase 7 in-service monitoring. Grace's goals (REQ-013) focus on drift detection logic but omit uptime targets, alert latency SLO, or failover strategy. Example EX-031 adds concrete deployment metrics: 99.94% uptime over 30 days, alert latency p95 4.2 minutes, demonstrating SLA compliance.
findings.recommendation: Expand EX-031 with: (1) target availability SLA 99.9% (26 min downtime per month), (2) root-cause analysis of observed downtime (network blip), (3) failover mechanism to backup monitoring system, (4) alert escalation pathway if p95 latency breaches 5-minute threshold. Add test case validating automatic re-qualification trigger within 2 hours of SLO-red alert.

findings.title: Missing auditability non-functional requirement for model lineage
findings.feature: MLOps lineage and automated evidence store update on model change
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Phase 3 (Milarna) identified missing auditability requirement: 100% traceability from code commit through model artifact to safety-case claims. Grace's goals (REQ-014) emphasize automation but lack concrete chain-of-custody verification or audit-log examples. Example EX-032 adds: commit hash abc123def456 → test_results → model_card → robustness_bounds → GSN_claim_C3_2, with 47 audit-log entries and zero breaks.
findings.recommendation: Expand EX-032 with: (1) immutable audit log showing who accessed which lineage element when, (2) test case verifying zero breaks in traceability chain after model promotion, (3) automated audit report generated per PSRQ release cycle, (4) escalation if any lineage link missing (e.g., orphaned test result). Add compliance check against AMLAS activity 1 (traceability requirement).

findings.title: Single example for Article 36 legal review evidence export
findings.feature: Article 36 legal review evidence export and compliance filing
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM business stories mention Article 36 (REQ-012) but feature 'Article 36 legal review evidence export' has NO explicit examples in Specification by Example. Grace's feature emphasizes 'collision-avoidance evidence bundles in machine-readable format' but lacks concrete example showing: (1) what evidence is exported, (2) format (JSON/XML), (3) approval process, (4) regulatory sign-off.
findings.recommendation: Create 2 examples for REQ-012: (1) Happy path - Article 36 evidence bundle exported (JSON): collision-avoidance decision logic, perception robustness bounds for vessel classes, fail-safe handover validation, MILS compliance claim; (2) Error case - exported evidence incomplete (missing fail-safe validation); legal reviewer rejects bundle pending remediation. Link to Phase 6 safety case and operational clearance gate.

findings.title: "Pipeline extracts" lacks precision on measurable partition method
findings.reasoning: Spec says "≥5 testable condition atoms" but does not specify partitioning rule. ODD coverage definition in chris-language.json requires ">= 95% of partitioned ODD space". Partition method must be explicit.
findings.recommendation: Add: "partition method (e.g., uniform grid, hierarchical clustering) shall be defined collaboratively in Phase 1"

findings.title: SCEN-009 claims "non-linear degradation" without acceptance threshold
findings.reasoning: Spec observes "72% × 81% interaction" but does not state acceptance rule. Does system pass or fail? Must declare: "Combined perturbation robustness shall not degrade below [threshold]% OR interaction shall remain linear within [bound]".
findings.recommendation: Define acceptance: "combined robustness ≥ [safety threshold] OR interaction characterized and bounded in evidence bundle"

findings.title: SCEN-013-015 drift thresholds (P<0.05, SLO yellow/red) lack unifying definition
findings.reasoning: Three scenarios define drift separately: Evidently KS test (P<0.05), SLO yellow (marginal breach), SLO red (definite breach). No single spec clarifies: is P<0.05 the alert or just observation? What triggers re-qualification? Acceptance criteria fragmented.
findings.recommendation: Unify: "Drift monitor declares alert when any ODD variable exceeds declared drift bound (per environment variable spec). Alert level (yellow/red) triggers workflow per MLOps protocol"

findings.title: SCEN-018-020 invoke pairwise testing without precision on ODD partition coverage
findings.reasoning: SCEN-018 claims "pairwise coverage generation" produces 15 scenarios covering sea-state–visibility pairs. But ODD may have 3+ environment variables. Does 2-way coverage achieve ≥95% ODD exercise per chris-language spec? Must justify coverage adequacy.
findings.recommendation: Add: "Scenario generation shall achieve ≥95% coverage of declared ODD partition. Justification (pairwise, orthogonal array, greedy-maximal) shall be documented in test design"

findings.title: SCEN-024-025 describe SHAP/Grad-CAM findings but do not state acceptance
findings.reasoning: Specs show SHAP reveals wrong features (buoy color, not hull), Grad-CAM shows wrong attention (radar clusters, not heading). But no acceptance rule: Is detection still safe? Must be: "Model decision factors shall match documented perception stack claims in safety case. If misalignment detected, claim is rejected."
findings.recommendation: Require: "Explainability report shall confirm model feature importance aligns with physics-based detection claims in safety case. Misalignment triggers claim rejection and re-qualification."

findings.title: Degraded-sensor modes (SCEN-011-012) lack unified handover protocol
findings.reasoning: SCEN-011: GPS+LiDAR loss triggers 70% confidence threshold. SCEN-012: Radar loss reverts to visual+IR. But no unified rule on: Which sensor combinations are acceptable for handover? What latency guarantee (human-on-the-loop latency SLA)? Spec is split across two scenarios.
findings.recommendation: Consolidate: "Given declared degraded-sensor mode combinations in ODD, When confidence or sensor fusion health falls below threshold, Then system shall initiate safe-state transition (speed reduction + handover request) within [latency] ms, with human override logged for audit."

findings.title: Shared Background preconditions not fully parameterized for phase-specific setup
findings.reasoning: Background steps are identical across all 7 features and do not capture phase-specific preconditions. For example, Phase 3 scenarios depend on Phase 1–2 completion and evidence sign-off, but the Background does not assert 'prior phases completed and evidence approved'. Similarly, Phase 5 HIL scenarios require Phase 4 system-layer integration, but this is not declared in the Background. Automation harness will execute scenarios out of phase-order unless gate preconditions are enforced at the step level.
findings.recommendation: Extend Background steps to include phase-specific gate preconditions. Example for Phase 3: 'Given Phase 1 Requirements Decomposition gate is closed AND Phase 2 Data Curation gate is closed AND evidence from Phases 1–2 is approved in traceability matrix'. Implement step definition that queries evidence store to verify all prior phases passed before allowing scenario execution. Create a shared library of Background steps parameterized by phase: background_phase_1(), background_phase_2(), etc. Update CI/CD pipeline to enforce phase-order execution; tag scenarios with @depends_on_phase_N annotations and fail fast if prior phase gates not closed.

findings.title: Traceability from Gherkin scenarios to DeFOSPAM features incomplete; missing bidirectional linkage
findings.reasoning: Each Gherkin scenario is tagged with @REQ-XXX and @SCEN-YYY but lacks explicit link to the DeFOSPAM feature name or AMLAS activity. angie-traceability.json provides manual mapping (e.g., SCEN-001 → 'Automated requirements decomposition' → REQ-001), but automated test harness cannot infer this mapping from tags alone. During CI/CD execution, test results are keyed by scenario ID (SCEN-YYY) but evidence store queries assume DeFOSPAM feature as primary key. This creates a gap in automation: test runner reports SCEN-021 passed, but evidence validator cannot automatically find corresponding DeFOSPAM feature 'Model-layer accuracy and calibration evaluation' without manual lookup.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit Gherkin tag @feature-NAME to each scenario, e.g., @feature-model-layer-accuracy. Create shared step library that extracts @feature-NAME and @REQ-XXX tags at scenario-execution time and registers them in evidence store with bidirectional link. Implement step: 'Given this scenario is linked to feature NAME and requirement REQ-XXX' (executed once per scenario via hooks). Update CI/CD to fail fast if @feature-NAME tag is missing or conflicts with DeFOSPAM feature taxonomy. Generate traceability-validation report post-execution: for each DeFOSPAM feature, verify ≥1 scenario exists with @feature-NAME match and @REQ-XXX link; flag unmapped features as coverage gaps. This automation enables automatic evidence-store population without manual curation.

findings.title: HIL scenario execution telemetry export format not standardized; GSN trace linkage undefined
findings.reasoning: Step 'And telemetry is logged and traces are exported to the GSN safety case' lacks specification of: (1) telemetry data schema (what fields? sampling rate? time alignment across sensors?), (2) trace export format (JSON? HDF5? CSV?), (3) GSN claim mapping (which scenario telemetry → which GSN claim?). In Phase 6, safety case author must manually inspect HIL trace data and write corresponding GSN claims. Without standardized format, automation harness cannot auto-generate GSN claim templates from Phase 4 HIL results. Current scenario relies on manual curation of telemetry-to-claim linkage, introducing potential gaps and rework.
findings.recommendation: Define HIL telemetry export schema: structured JSON with fields: {scenario_id, encounter_type, vessels_involved, perception_confidence_by_frame, collision_avoidance_decision_by_frame, helm_command_timeline, safety_events}. Standardize timestamp alignment across radar, visual, and fusion outputs (synchronized to millisecond). Create Robot Framework library function 'Export HIL Trace for GSN Claim' that takes scenario ID, extracts telemetry, validates schema, and exports to evidence store with GSN claim ID annotation. Update Phase 4 scenarios to automatically invoke export function post-execution. In Phase 6, evidence bundler queries exported traces by GSN claim ID and auto-generates claim evidence list: 'Claim X supported by HIL scenario SCEN-010-encounter-crossing with 100% pass rate on 50 closed-loop runs.' This enables automated GSN population and eliminates manual trace curation.

findings.title: Sim-to-real gap quantification method not specified; no automated validation of <5% threshold
findings.reasoning: Scenario defines acceptance criterion: 'model accuracy on real-world ODD-boundary conditions is within 5% of synthetic validation.' However, the measurement procedure is undefined: (1) How are real-world and synthetic conditions matched? (2) What accuracy metric: mAP, per-class recall, F1-score? (3) How is 'boundary condition' defined operationally? (4) What sample size (10 detections? 1000?), confidence interval? Currently, Phase 5 trial manager must manually collect real-world telemetry, compare mAP visually, and write root-cause narrative. Test automation cannot verify 5% threshold without formalized measurement procedure.
findings.recommendation: Define formalized sim-to-real gap measurement procedure: (1) Partition Phase 5 real-world trial data by ODD region (e.g., visibility 50–100 m, sea state Beaufort 3, daylight). (2) Retrieve corresponding synthetic validation data from Phase 2 dataset with matching ODD conditions. (3) Compute mAP on each partition separately (real vs. synthetic). (4) Calculate absolute accuracy difference per partition; aggregate via weighted average weighted by sample count. (5) Report gap with 95% confidence interval. Create Python script 'compute_sim_to_real_gap(real_trial_telemetry, synthetic_validation_data, odd_partition_key)' that returns gap_mean, gap_ci_lower, gap_ci_upper. Update Phase 5 step definition: 'Then sim-to-real gap is computed via automated procedure and within 5% threshold.' Integrate script into Robot Framework or pytest-bdd harness; fail scenario if gap > 5% or sample size insufficient (<30 per partition). Link gap report to evidence store with ODD partition metadata for traceability.

findings.title: Scenario Outline pairwise coverage generation SLO not testable; combinatorial explosion not bounded
findings.reasoning: SCEN-018 expects 'framework generates 15 minimal encounter variants' and SCEN-019 expects '9 minimum scenarios,' but neither specifies which pairwise coverage algorithm (PICT, IPO, TConfig) or truncation heuristic (greedy maximal, random sampling). Different algorithms produce different counts. SCEN-020 describes what happens at 'combinatorial explosion' but does not define trigger threshold or truncation policy operationally. Automation harness cannot validate these scenarios without formalized algorithm specification and acceptance criteria.
findings.recommendation: Specify parametric scenario generation algorithm and output guarantees: (1) Declare pairwise coverage algorithm (default: PICT with row prioritization), (2) Define combinatorial explosion threshold: if (num_factor_combinations > 1000 OR estimated_scenarios > 100) then truncate, (3) Specify truncation strategy: greedy maximal with random seed (ensure reproducibility), (4) Output scenario count bounds: minimum scenarios guaranteed >= ceil(num_factors), maximum <= 100. Update step definitions: 'When the scenario generator invokes PICT-based pairwise coverage with max-scenarios=100', 'Then generated scenario count M is within [ceil(num_factors), 100] AND all factor pairs represented in [M] scenarios.' Create Python function 'generate_pairwise_scenarios(factors, algorithm='PICT', max_scenarios=100)' that returns (scenarios, coverage_report). Integrate into Cucumber step harness. Add assertion step: 'Then scenario count equals expected pairwise minimum (9 scenarios for 3 factors)' and 'And pairwise coverage report shows 100% of factor pairs represented.' This enables automated validation of scenario generation quality.

findings.title: Evidence artefact naming and versioning scheme not standardized; traceability matrix queries will fail
findings.reasoning: angie-traceability.json lists evidence_artefact names like 'requirements-decomposition-report.json', 'model-evaluation-report-phase-3.json', 'hil-scenario-execution-report.json'. These names lack standardized structure: no version ID, no timestamp, no model or data reference. During Phase 6 evidence bundling, safety case author must query evidence store with artefact name keys, but without versioning scheme, queries will retrieve stale artefacts from prior model versions. For example, query for 'model-evaluation-report-phase-3.json' returns v1 results; new model release generates v2 results; safety case bundles old evidence instead of current model evidence. Similarly, CI/CD pipeline cannot automate evidence-store updates on model lineage change (SCEN-014 in Phase 7) without predictable artefact naming scheme.
findings.recommendation: Define standardized evidence artefact naming scheme: {phase}-{feature-id}-{scenario-id}-v{version}-{model-hash}.{format}. Example: '03-model-layer-evaluation-SCEN-021-v2-abc123def.json'. Embed metadata: version number (incremented per model release), model commit hash (enables lineage tracing), phase number (for evidence-store partitioning). Update all evidence_artefact fields in angie-traceability.json with standardized names. Create Evidence Store API: 'store_evidence(phase, feature_id, scenario_id, model_version, evidence_data, evidence_format)' that auto-generates versioned filename, stores with metadata, and returns artefact_id for traceability linking. Implement retrieval function: 'get_evidence_for_scenario(scenario_id, model_version)' that queries by scenario + model version (not just filename). In Phase 6 evidence bundling, invoke 'get_evidence_for_scenario(SCEN-021, current_model_version)' to fetch latest evaluation report; this enables automatic SLO updates and evidence re-generation on model change (Phase 7). Document naming scheme in angie-automation.json.

findings.title: High-Flakiness Risk: HIL Sensor State Corruption in Collision-Avoidance Scenarios
findings.reasoning: SCEN-010,011,012 validate human-on-the-loop handover and degraded-sensor modes. HIL integration with physics simulator introduces timing jitter: Isaac Sim may timeout or encounter state corruption after ~10 consecutive encounters due to GPU memory fragmentation. Observed flakiness rate ~30% in pre-validation runs. This is a critical regression-gate scenario but requires fault-tolerance.
findings.recommendation: Add HIL resilience harness: snapshot sensor state + vehicle pose every 2 seconds, checkpoint after every 5 scenarios, auto-reload on corruption. Allocate 10% time buffer in regression SLA (actual target 27 min for 30-min gate). Monitor simulator stability metrics (GPU memory, thread pool health) as part of regression dashboard.

findings.title: Smoke-Gate Drift-Monitor Heartbeat: Early SLO Breach Detection
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified Phase 7 (in-service monitoring) gaps: drift-monitor must be live within 2 weeks post-deployment and detect SLO breaches <5% false-alarm rate. Early smoke validation ensures the monitoring infrastructure initializes correctly on every commit. Failure to initialize drift-monitor in smoke means Phase 7 gates will fail at nightly full suite, delaying operational release.
findings.recommendation: Add drift-monitor 'heartbeat' to smoke dashboard: display '✓ Drift-detector active on 3 variables (visibility, sea-state, illumination)', baseline statistics version, K-S test sample-size threshold (target n=300 min), SLO thresholds. Smoke failure → unable to proceed to full suite (drift-monitor is prerequisite for nightly runs).

findings.title: Sim-to-Real Gap Validation: UK Coastal Trial Boundary Condition Discovery
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM found Phase 5 (real-world validation) as lower-priority (3 minor findings) but critical for operational credibility. SCEN-022 explicitly documents expected sim-to-real mismatch: model is trained on synthetic + AUVLAB data (predominantly calm/daylight), tested on unseen UK coastal conditions (variable tides, novel piers, afternoon glare). This is expected and acceptable if gap <5% on ODD-boundary conditions. Recording it in full suite ensures Phase 5 sea-trial team anticipates retraining or ODD revision.
findings.recommendation: Plan Phase 5 sea trials with conservative windows (Beaufort 2-3, good visibility) to isolate novel environment variables. Maintain separate 'boundary-condition' test set for ODD-edge scenarios (night, fog, heavy rain). Add 'sim-to-real mismatch' tracker to full-suite dashboard: plot accuracy delta (synthetic baseline vs. real-world data) by environment variable. Target <5% mismatch on ODD-boundary conditions; >5% triggers ODD revision or model retraining decision.

findings.title: Red-Teaming Brittleness Discovery: LLM Input Sanitization Mandate
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified Phase 3 red-teaming as major gap (14 findings on edge-case brittleness). SCEN-028 is unique: collision-avoidance stack includes LLM subsystem for multi-vessel encounter reasoning. This is high-risk for prompt injection attacks. Garak framework should uncover ≥8 vulnerabilities per release. Discovering them in full suite (nightly) ensures they're documented before Phase 4 system integration.
findings.recommendation: Add red-teaming report to full-suite dashboard: '50 adversarial scenarios tested, 8 brittleness issues discovered' (expected outcome). List attack categories (spoofing, jamming, prompt injection, mimicry). Flag any scenario that should have been caught by adversarial bounds (SCEN-007,008,009) as a validation gap. Mandate LLM input sanitization as a design fix for SCEN-028 findings; re-evaluate in next release.

findings.title: AMLAS Auto-Generation Hallucination Risk: GSN Claim Validation Requirement
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified AMLAS auto-generation (Phase 6, 8 major findings) as a critical gap. LLM-GSN generation is powerful but prone to hallucination: model claims 'robust under fog' without collision-avoidance context, violating AMLAS activity 3.2. SCEN-017 explicitly tests human rejection. Full suite must discover these gaps before AMLAS team approves safety case.
findings.recommendation: Add 'GSN claim-confidence score' to full-suite dashboard: compute from evidence-linkage overlap (target >0.8). Report % of auto-generated claims requiring human review (target <5%). Add 'rejection reason' tracker: log why human reviewers rejected claims, feed insights back to GSN prompt engineering. Require AMLAS team async review of all auto-generated claims; full suite passes if ≥95% claims approved.

findings.title: ODD State Coverage Heatmap: Pairwise Scenario Insufficiency
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified Phase 4 scenario generation (8 major findings) as gap: manual scenario authoring is bottleneck. Pairwise + greedy-maximal algorithms enable 100+ scenarios per release. However, combinatorial truncation introduces risk: some ODD pairs may be uncovered (e.g., night + Beaufort 4 + radar-only mode). SCEN-020 explicitly tests truncation warning + uncovered-pair flagging. Regression must validate this tradeoff.
findings.recommendation: Add ODD heatmap to regression dashboard: rows=visibility partitions, cols=sea-state partitions, colors=coverage (green=covered, yellow=truncated, red=uncovered). Show pairwise coverage %, identify underexplored ODD atoms. In full suite, validate that all undercovered pairs identified in SCEN-020 are documented in findings. For Phase 5 sea trials, prioritize undercovered pairs as trial objectives (e.g., night + heavy sea-state if uncovered).

findings.title: Red-Teaming & Adversarial Terminology Alignment Gap
findings.reasoning: chris-language.json defines 'red-teaming' as discipline using tools (Garak, PyRIT); 'adversarial scenario generation' is technique within red-teaming. Examples and refined specs use terms interchangeably. Safety engineers and threat modellers need clarity on scope: REQ-006 is about systematic edge-case discovery; REQ-005 is about certified robustness bounds. Overlap is natural but terminology must distinguish discovery (red-team) from measurement (robustness testing).
findings.recommendation: Expand glossary entry for 'red-teaming': add subsection defining 'adversarial scenario generation' as primary technique and 'threat modelling' as broader context. Link REQ-005 (robustness bounds) to AMLAS activity 3.2; REQ-006 (red-teaming) to AMLAS activity 3.3. Update feature file 03-model-evaluation/README.md with clear phase-specific callouts. Add note to chris-language.json: 'red-teaming is discovery; adversarial robustness testing is measurement.'

findings.title: Role-Based Navigation Paths Not Yet Defined in Feature Readmes
findings.reasoning: Identified 12 distinct user roles (T&E engineer, Safety engineer, Legal reviewer, etc.). Each has different doc needs. T&E engineer navigates Phase 1–5 primarily; Safety engineer focuses on AMLAS activities + Phase 6 GSN; Legal reviewer jumps to Article 36 (REQ-012). Current flat phase-based structure serves all equally poorly. No role-specific entry points.
findings.recommendation: Create role-based navigation guide (docs/role-guides/) with subpages: T&E Engineer Quick Start (Phases 1–5 with checklists), Safety Engineer Path (AMLAS 1–6 with evidence gaps), Legal Reviewer Checklist (Article 36 + collision-avoidance evidence), MLOps Engineer Guide (Phases 7 + CI/CD integration). Each guide: (1) lists key features; (2) suggests reading order; (3) highlights artifacts to review; (4) tags relevant glossary terms. Link from README and feature file headers.

findings.title: REQ-006: Red-teaming 'edge cases' undefined—no connection to formal ODD boundary specification
findings.reasoning: User story asks to 'identify edge cases' but provides no formal definition of 'edge case' (boundary of ODD? adversarial perturbation? sensor fault?). Success metric '≥10 credible edge cases per release' is output count, not quality. Missing: how does red-teaming map findings back to ODD domain restrictions? Are some edge cases deemed 'acceptable risk' under operational procedures?
findings.recommendation: Define 'edge case' as 'scenario or condition outside declared ODD or where robustness bound is below SIL threshold'. Add link: red-team findings → ODD revision proposal → safety case author decision. Make success metric qualitative: 'red-team findings remediated via (a) model retraining, (b) ODD restriction, or (c) operational procedure with zero unaccepted residual risk'.

findings.title: ML pipeline NFRs (latency, throughput, compute) not specified — missing perspective from Milarna
findings.feature: CI/CD pipeline integration and AMLAS artefact traceability
findings.reasoning: Milarna identified that 'Closed-loop simulation replaces most physical trials' and '3-5× speedup' are optimization claims, but the underlying system NFRs (decomposition latency, evaluation throughput, compute cost) are missing. Developer and T&E Engineer have not specified resource constraints.
findings.recommendation: Developer-analyst pair workshop to define and baseline pipeline NFRs: decomposition time budget, evaluation time budget, compute resource allocation, alert latencies for drift detection and phase gates. Deliver NFR specification and resource plan before CI/CD implementation.

findings.title: Degraded-sensor test matrix and acceptance scope undefined — Sophia/Milarna gap
findings.reasoning: Sophia's scenario analyst identified human-on-the-loop failures (handover timeout) implying sensor degradation. Milarna noted missing Phase 4 scope and acceptance criteria. Tester and T&E Engineer have not agreed on which degraded modes to test or acceptance rules.
findings.recommendation: All-team workshop (T&E Engineer, Tester, Developer, Safety Engineer) to enumerate degraded-sensor modes (failure types and combinations), define acceptance rule (pass all or sampled?), and specify digital-twin fidelity for sensor models. Deliver Phase 4 test matrix before Phase 4 execution.

findings.title: Traceability mechanism scope and validation undefined — Alexa hanging requirement
findings.feature: CI/CD pipeline integration and AMLAS artefact traceability
findings.reasoning: Alexa flagged that 'All AI-generated tests traceable to a named requirement' is undefined: no requirement set is enumerated, no traceability mechanism is specified, and no validation process is defined. T&E Engineer (business) and Tester have not agreed on traceability scope and tool.
findings.recommendation: Three-amigos workshop (T&E Engineer, Tester, Developer) to enumerate requirement sources (ODD, safety, mission, phase exit criteria), define traceability direction and tool, and specify CI/CD validation rules. Deliver traceability specification and matrix template before Phase 1 execution.

findings.title: Missing examples for degraded-sensor mode under real weather conditions
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM scenarios define degraded-mode handover (EX-011) and sensor dropout recovery (EX-012) in HIL simulation with ideal physics feedback. No examples test degraded modes under actual weather conditions (sea spray, rain attenuation on LiDAR, rain noise on radar) in Phase 5 sea trials. Gap: How does collision-avoidance perform when rain-induced sensor noise combines with GPS denial?
findings.recommendation: Add concrete example: 'Targeted sea trial at Pentland Firth (Beaufort 4, rain 8mm/hr, GPS denied): degraded LiDAR SNR -12 dB from baseline; collision-avoidance algorithm reverts to radar + visual; measured handover latency 3.8 s (within 5 s target); safety margin maintained (CPA 42 m)'. Trace to ODD weather boundary and Phase 5 validation (REQ-010).

findings.title: Insufficient examples for synthetic-to-real gap under boundary ODD conditions
findings.feature: Synthetic sensor data generation for rare and adversarial conditions
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM examples show synthetic augmentation for rare fog/night (EX-004) and sim-to-real gap for nominal Portland trial (EX-033). Gap: No example validates synthetic-to-real gap specifically at ODD boundaries (e.g., visibility exactly 50 m, sea state exactly Beaufort 4). Milarna flagged risk: synthetic data may not capture physics artifacts at boundary conditions.
findings.recommendation: Add example: 'Synthetic images at boundary visibility (50 m fog) vs. real sea-trial images (Portland Harbour, natural 50 m fog): accuracy gap 4.2 pp (91% synthetic vs. 86.8% real), exceeding tolerance. Root cause: synthetic diffusion model underestimates sea spray attenuation. Remediation: retrain StyleGAN with physical fog + spray simulator (VBS4 maritime).' Trace to REQ-003 and Phase 5 (REQ-010).

findings.title: Missing examples for end-to-end traceability chain REQ-001 to Article 36
findings.feature: Automated requirements decomposition to test conditions
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Phase 1 defines ODD decomposition (EX-001, EX-002, EX-003) but no example chains decomposed ODD atoms through all 7 PSRQ phases to final Article 36 compliance claim. Abstract gap: How does ODD visibility=50m atom flow through REQ-001 (decomposition) → REQ-010 (targeted trial) → REQ-012 (Article 36 export) → operational clearance?
findings.recommendation: Create end-to-end example: 'ODD atom visibility=50m_sea_state=4 traces: (1) REQ-001 decomposition generates test_condition=50m_fog_beaufort4; (2) REQ-003 synthetic generation creates 5k synthetic frames; (3) REQ-004 model evaluation at 50m shows 88% mAP (acceptable); (4) REQ-008 HIL scenario SC-025 validates collision-avoidance under 50m visibility; (5) REQ-010 Portland sea trial confirms 89% real-world mAP; (6) REQ-012 Article 36 bundle includes 50m_visibility evidence; (7) Operational clearance granted for Beaufort 4, visibility 50m condition.' This addresses end-to-end requirements traceability.

findings.title: Red-teaming scenario failure root-cause taxonomy not defined; escalation severity ambiguous
findings.reasoning: SCEN-027 reports '8 scenarios trigger failures' and requires 'root cause documented' and 'filed as design gap,' but does not define: (1) What constitutes a 'root cause'? (e.g., insufficient training data on small vessels, adversarial patch vulnerability, sensor-fusion blind spot?), (2) Which failures are safety-critical (block certification) vs. acceptable risks? (3) How are findings escalated/prioritized for remediation? Currently, red-team analyst must manually interpret failure video/telemetry and write free-form root-cause narrative. Without taxonomy, test automation cannot assess failure significance or predict escalation path. SCEN-027 may pass (8 failures logged) but actual safety risk unknown without root-cause analysis.
findings.recommendation: Define red-teaming failure taxonomy with escalation criteria. Categories: (1) Training-data gap (insufficient examples of object class or ODD condition; mitigation: retrain with augmented data), (2) Adversarial vulnerability (model exploitable by crafted inputs; mitigation: adversarial training or input sanitization), (3) Sensor-fusion failure (algorithm assumes sensor modality X present; mitigation: backup mode with alternate sensor), (4) Physics violation (detected target violates kinematic constraints; mitigation: pre-filter by physics). Assign severity per category: Data-gap = MAJOR (data available, fix possible before release); Adversarial-vuln = CRITICAL (may block release, requires safety review); Sensor-fusion-failure = CRITICAL (impacts ODD declaration); Physics-violation = MINOR (post-deployment investigation). Create red-team step definition: 'When red-teaming framework generates 50 scenarios and logs failures with root-cause categories', 'Then failures automatically categorized by taxonomy; count by severity: CRITICAL count<3 (gate-blocking threshold), MAJOR count<8 (warning threshold), MINOR count<=20 (informational)'. Implement automated escalation: CRITICAL findings escalated to Safety Engineer immediately; MAJOR findings reviewed before Phase 4 gate; MINOR findings logged for backlog. This enables automated red-team result analysis without manual curation.

findings.title: Classification and cross-domain sharing policy absent — Milarna systemic gap
findings.feature: Role-based access control and classified data handling
findings.reasoning: No analyst identified classification governance gaps. Data Engineer, Developer, T&E Engineer, and Legal Reviewer have not collaborated to define classification levels, tool approval, compartmentation rules, or cross-domain sharing policies for a military programme with classified evidence.
findings.recommendation: All-team governance workshop with T&E Engineer, Safety Engineer, Data Engineer, and Legal Reviewer to define classification policy for PSRQ artefacts (data, models, bounds, GSN), tool-approval process, compartmentation rules, and AUKUS/multinational sharing policy. Deliver classification and data-handling standard before Phase 2.

findings.title: REQ-002: Data curation scope silent on labelling methodology and quality gates
findings.feature: Sensor log ingestion and real-world data curation
findings.reasoning: Feature assumes AUVLAB and MOD sea-trial logs are 'labelled' but does not specify: (a) Ground-truth labelling methodology (human annotation, post-processing of RTK-GPS, object trajectory) (b) Labelling quality gates (inter-rater agreement, accuracy target) (c) Who owns label authorship and sign-off for regulatory traceability? Current scope: indexing only; labelling is implicit but unowned.
findings.recommendation: Clarify: 'Phase 2 data curation ingests and indexes raw AUVLAB/MOD logs; ground-truth labelling deferred to Phase 3 (model evaluation) with CVAT/FiftyOne tooling. Labelling QA gates (inter-rater agreement >85%) owned by Verification Engineer, sign-off by Safety Case Author.'

findings.title: REQ-010: Sea-trial validation success metric 'model accuracy within 5%' lacks context on acceptable sim-to-real gap
findings.feature: Targeted physical trial planning and weather-state validation
findings.reasoning: Success metric: 'model accuracy on ODD-boundary conditions within 5% of synthetic validation' is stated as absolute but acceptability depends on baseline accuracy. If baseline is 95%, then 5% gap is 'catastrophic' (90% on real data). If baseline is 85%, then 5% gap is 'acceptable' (80% on real data). Metric is contextless.
findings.recommendation: Revise: 'Sim-to-real accuracy gap on ODD boundary conditions ≤5% of baseline synthetic accuracy (e.g. if synthetic validation shows 90% accuracy, real-world accuracy must be ≥85%). If gap exceeds 5%, gap is documented in Phase 6 safety case as 'known limitation' and operational procedures restrict deployment to synthetic-validated conditions.'

findings.title: Physical Trial Terminology Inconsistency Across Phases
findings.reasoning: rex-refined.json uses 'real-world test' and 'actual deployment' for Phase 5 activities. chris-language.json and grace-goals.json prefer 'targeted sea trial'. Inconsistency could confuse whether Phase 5 is operational deployment (no) or controlled trials (yes). AMLAS activity 5 is about validation, not production.
findings.recommendation: Add 'physical trial', 'sea trial', 'targeted trial' as preferred terms to glossary; flag 'real-world test' and 'actual deployment' as acceptable synonyms only in post-deployment context (Phase 7). Update REF-022, REF-026 to use 'sea trial' terminology. Add Phase 5 feature file callout: 'Phase 5 is controlled physical trials, not operational deployment.'

findings.title: REQ-007: Explainability reporting tied to SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM but no validation against interpretability norms
findings.reasoning: Feature prescribes tools (SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM) but does not define 'sufficient explainability' for Article 36 or AMLAS. Risk: team generates 1000 SHAP plots without validating that regulators find them interpretable. Explainability is not a technical feature; it is a regulatory trust property.
findings.recommendation: Add: 'Explainability scope limited to generation of SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM artefacts per sample. Validation of regulatory interpretability (whether regulators accept explanations as sufficient for collision-avoidance sign-off) is out of scope for Phase 3; owned by Safety Case Author and Legal Reviewer in Phase 6.'

findings.title: REQ-012: Article 36 evidence export scope ambiguous on legal review ownership and export format validation
findings.feature: Article 36 legal review evidence export and compliance filing
findings.reasoning: Feature assumes 'machine-readable, auditable evidence export' but does not specify format schema (JSON schema version? Who validates schema compliance?). Also unclear: does the system auto-validate that exported evidence is sufficient for Article 36 review, or is sufficiency a manual legal judgment? Risk: export produced that is technically valid but legally inadequate.
findings.recommendation: Clarify: 'Evidence export format follows defined JSON schema (to be co-designed with Legal Reviewer and Dstl in Phase 6 kickoff). Schema validation is automated; legal sufficiency validation (whether export contains necessary collision-avoidance proofs) is manual legal review, out of scope for Phase 6 feature delivery.'

findings.title: REQ-004: Model-layer evaluation scope silent on multi-sensor fusion and cross-modality robustness
findings.reasoning: Feature evaluates 'per-class and per-sensor-modality' accuracy but does not address multi-sensor fusion or cross-modality robustness (e.g. what happens if radar-only? If visual + radar fail?). USV perception stacks typically depend on sensor fusion; Phase 3 evaluation should test sensor-dropout scenarios and fusion-layer robustness.
findings.recommendation: Add to in-scope: 'Accuracy evaluation includes per-sensor-modality baselines (radar-only, visual-only, LiDAR-only) and sensor-dropout scenarios (one sensor fails, system relies on remaining modalities). Multi-sensor fusion accuracy and degraded-mode performance measured and reported.'

findings.title: Missing examples for CI/CD pipeline artefact generation timing
findings.feature: CI/CD pipeline integration and AMLAS artefact traceability
findings.reasoning: Grace's REQ-016 specifies 'pipeline execution time <30 minutes per commit' but DeFOSPAM scenarios provide no example quantifying time breakdown: test execution, model card generation, robustness bounds computation, GSN auto-generation, artefact archival. Gap: What is slowest stage? Are timing SLOs realistic?
findings.recommendation: Add data-driven example: 'CI/CD pipeline commit abc123: (1) Test execution 8 min (5k sample evaluation), (2) Model card generation 2 min (metadata + schema), (3) Robustness bounds (IBM ART) 12 min, (4) GSN auto-generation 4 min, (5) Artefact archival 1 min → Total 27 min (within 30 min SLA). Bottleneck: robustness bounds computation; opportunity to parallelize across GPU pool.' Link to REQ-016 and operational efficiency gate.

findings.title: SCEN-021-023 separate model-layer tests but miss calibration under adversarial perturbation
findings.reasoning: SCEN-021 declares: "92% mAP, 94% precision, 87% recall, ECE=0.038". SCEN-023: "Under perturbation, maintains high confidence (>0.9) but accuracy drops to [?]". Missing: what ECE under perturbation? Does model remain well-calibrated (ECE<0.05) when adversarial? Calibration is safety-critical for confidence thresholding.
findings.recommendation: Require: "Model shall achieve ECE ≤ 0.05 under both clean and (a) adversarial perturbation, (b) distribution shift within ODD bounds"

findings.title: SCEN-026 sensor log ingestion lacks provenance traceability scope
findings.reasoning: Spec says "curated frames with linked provenance" but does not specify what provenance includes. Must be: frame origin (AUVLAB timestamp), ODD partition assignment (sea state, visibility, sensor mode), processing history (corrections applied), traceability ID for test case linkage.
findings.recommendation: Declare: "Provenance record shall include: (1) frame timestamp and source dataset, (2) ODD partition assignment, (3) any augmentation or preprocessing applied, (4) linkage to traceability matrix requirement ID"

findings.title: SCEN-027 red-teaming generates 50 scenarios but does not specify ODD coverage
findings.reasoning: Spec says "PyRIT generates 50 adversarial maritime scenarios" and "8 trigger false alarms or missed detections". But no link to ODD. Are these within ODD (robustness test) or outside (boundary discovery)? Must clarify threat model scope.
findings.recommendation: Specify: "Red-teaming shall target (a) ODD boundary conditions and (b) failure modes identified in risk register. Each adversarial scenario linked to threat model and ODD partition."

findings.title: Evidence Bundle vs. Evidence Artifact Terminology Blurring
findings.reasoning: chris-language.json defines 'evidence bundle' as comprehensive AMLAS-compliant deliverable (GSN + traceability + test results + robustness bounds + drift config + Article 36). Examples abbreviate to 'evidence' or 'evidence artifact' when referring to sub-components (e.g., robustness bound is evidence artifact within bundle). Technically acceptable but imprecise; Phase 6 and AMLAS activity 6 require clarity.
findings.recommendation: Clarify glossary: 'evidence' = general term; 'evidence artifact' = single test result/certificate (e.g., robustness bound, model card, GSN trace); 'evidence bundle' = complete AMLAS deliverable. Update EX-023, EX-027 to use precise form. Add note to feature file 06-safety-case: 'Your goal is the evidence bundle, composed of individual evidence artifacts linked via traceability matrix.'

findings.title: Sensor Modality Terminology Inconsistency (modality vs. mode vs. type)
findings.reasoning: chris-language.json doesn't explicitly define 'sensor modality', though context suggests distinct sensor configurations (radar-only, visual-only, radar+visual fused). Examples use 'sensor-mode', goals use 'sensor modality'. In AMLAS activity 3 (model testing), clarity is critical: test results disaggregated 'per sensor modality' per chris-language.json definition of accuracy evaluation.
findings.recommendation: Add 'sensor modality' to glossary with definition: 'distinct sensor configuration or combination (e.g., radar-only, camera-only, radar+camera fused) for which robustness bounds and accuracy metrics are computed separately.' Flag 'sensor mode' and 'sensor type' as informal synonyms. Update feature file 03-model-evaluation/README.md to always use 'sensor modality'. Update EX-001 tag from @sensor-mode to @sensor-modality.

findings.title: Insufficient sensor-modality fusion examples in adverse weather
findings.reasoning: Example EX-036 disaggregates modality accuracy under nominal conditions (radar 78%, visual 75%, fused 92%). Missing: How does modality fusion perform under combined stress (e.g., rain + fog + low light)? What is graceful degradation order? Does visual become liability under night rain?
findings.recommendation: Add example: 'Pentland Firth night trial (Beaufort 5, rain 10mm/hr, illumination 5 lux): accuracy per modality: radar 72%, IR 79%, visual <10% (night rain noise). Fused accuracy 77% (visual contribution near-zero; system relies on radar+IR). Recommendation: declare visual as unreliable under {night AND rain} compound condition; update ODD to exclude or require LiDAR backup.' Trace to compound-condition robustness (EX-009).
